description: Detect language engineered to coerce, shame, silence, or psychologically control the reader or a referenced target. These patterns go beyond poor argument — they are tools of interpersonal or institutional power abuse.

rules.name: Guilt-Tripping / Shame Weaponization
patterns: how could you do this to
I thought you were better than this
and this is the thanks I get
rules.explanation: Uses guilt or shame as a lever to control behavior. The speaker positions themselves as the wronged party and the target as morally deficient. The goal is compliance through emotional debt, not resolution through dialogue. Look for appeals to obligation that bypass the actual merits of the disagreement.

rules.name: Gaslighting / Reality Denial
rules.explanation: Systematically denies the target's perception of reality to make them doubt their own memory, judgment, or sanity. The pattern is: target states experience → speaker flatly denies it → target is framed as confused or unstable. Repeated exposure erodes the target's self-trust. Look for blanket denials paired with characterizations of the target as irrational.

rules.name: Silencing / Discourse Shutdown
patterns: I don't want to hear it
that's just the way it is
you don't get a say in this
rules.explanation: Shuts down dialogue through authority, dismissal, or refusal to engage. The target's right to question, object, or contribute is revoked. Unlike ending a genuinely unproductive conversation, silencing is unilateral and often deployed the moment the speaker faces a point they cannot counter. Look for flat refusals to engage that come at the moment of challenge, not after genuine deliberation.

rules.name: Threats / Intimidation
patterns: you don't want to go there
you have no idea what I'm capable of
think very carefully about your next move
rules.explanation: Implicit or explicit threats designed to produce compliance through fear. The threatened consequences may be social (exposure, reputation damage), professional (firing, demotion), relational (abandonment), or left deliberately vague to maximize anxiety. Look for future-tense warnings where the speaker positions themselves as the agent of the consequence.

rules.name: Obligation / Loyalty Weaponization
patterns: I've always been there for you
rules.explanation: Weaponizes love, loyalty, duty, or sacrifice to coerce compliance. The target's identity (as partner, parent, friend) is held hostage — comply or lose the identity label. The actual request is never evaluated on merit; it's wrapped in an identity test. Look for conditional identity statements: 'if you were really X, you would Y.'

rules.name: Identity / Character Assassination
patterns: this is who you really are
everyone can see what you are
rules.explanation: Attacks the target's core identity rather than a specific behavior. 'You forgot the appointment' (behavior) vs 'you're irresponsible' (identity). Identity attacks are global, stable, and internal — they frame the flaw as permanent and inherent. Look for 'you ARE' statements (identity) where 'you DID' statements (behavior) would be factual.

rules.name: Isolation Language
patterns: no one else would put up with you
rules.explanation: Cuts off the target's support network by asserting that everyone else agrees with the speaker or that nobody else would tolerate the target. Creates dependency by framing the speaker as the only source of acceptance. Look for claims about unanimous agreement, assertions that the target is universally disliked, or statements that frame the speaker as the target's sole ally.

rules.name: Martyrdom / Weaponized Victimhood
patterns: I guess I'm always the bad guy
nothing I do is ever good enough
fine, I'll just suffer in silence
I guess my feelings don't matter
go ahead, blame me for everything
don't worry about me, I'll manage
no one appreciates what I do
rules.explanation: The speaker preemptively positions themselves as the victim to deflect accountability and shift the emotional burden onto the target. The target must now comfort the speaker instead of addressing the original issue. Look for performative suffering that redirects the conversation from the speaker's behavior to the speaker's feelings.

rules.name: Weaponized Questions
patterns: why do you always have to
why do you make everything about
how many times do I have to
why would you even think that
rules.explanation: Questions designed not to seek information but to frame the target as deficient. 'Why are you getting defensive?' presupposes defensiveness and frames calm disagreement as pathological. Any answer validates the embedded accusation. Look for questions that contain their own verdict — the 'question' is actually a judgment wearing a question mark.

rules.name: Minimizing / Invalidation
patterns: you're blowing this out of proportion
rules.explanation: Dismisses or downgrades the target's legitimate experience or emotional response. The speaker unilaterally decides what the target is 'allowed' to feel. 'It was just a joke' after causing harm retroactively reframes the act as harmless and the target as humorless. Look for one person dictating the severity of another person's experience.

rules.name: Conditional Affection / Love Bombing
patterns: if you cared about me you'd
I'm only saying this because I love you
this hurts me more than it hurts you
I wouldn't have to do this if you'd just
I'm doing this because I care
I only want what's best for you
rules.explanation: Uses affection, care, or love as a transactional lever. Affection is offered conditionally — comply and receive love, resist and it's withdrawn. 'I'm only saying this because I love you' frames the attack as care, making the target feel ungrateful for objecting. Look for affection language immediately adjacent to demands or criticism.

rules.name: DARVO (Deny, Attack, Reverse Victim/Offender)
patterns: I never did that, and you're the one who
I'm not the problem, you are
look what you made me do
I wouldn't have had to if you hadn't
rules.explanation: A three-stage manipulation: (1) Deny the behavior, (2) Attack the person who called it out, (3) Reverse roles so the offender becomes the victim. The original target is reframed as the aggressor. Look for sequences where an accusation is met with denial immediately followed by a counter-accusation that flips who is harming whom.

rules.name: Catastrophizing for Control
patterns: everything is falling apart because of you
there's no coming back from this
nothing will ever be the same
rules.explanation: Inflates the consequences of the target's action to apocalyptic proportions to paralyze them with guilt or fear. A minor disagreement becomes 'destroying the family.' The exaggerated stakes make the target retreat to compliance to avoid the catastrophe. Look for permanence language ('irreparable', 'never', 'destroyed') applied to recoverable situations.

rules.name: Dehumanizing Language
rules.explanation: Strips humanity from a target group using animal, insect, disease, or filth metaphors. Dehumanization is the linguistic precursor to justifying violence, exclusion, or rights removal. Once a group is 'vermin' rather than people, normal moral constraints feel less applicable. Look for biological or pest metaphors applied to human groups.

rules.name: Weaponized Generosity / Debt Creation
patterns: I've done so much for you
you wouldn't be where you are without me
I put you where you are
I was there when no one else was
everything you have is because of me
don't forget what I did for you
rules.explanation: Past favors or generosity are retroactively converted into debts that demand repayment through compliance. What appeared to be freely given now has strings attached. Look for historical kindness cited not in gratitude but as leverage — the subtext is 'you owe me, therefore you must comply.'
